title: Does `skopeo copy` support increment transfer?
body:
Suppose I have an image name `example-base-image:tag`, and I saved it in my own docker-registry.

Then I use the image as base image to create a new one, the docker file like below

```dockerfile
FROM example-base-image:tag
COPY . /app
```
My question is when I use `skopeo copy` to transfer the image to my own docker-registry, does it transfer all layers of the image or just the last layer?

If I try to encrypt the image and push to my own docker-registry, should I encrypt the whole image or use `--encrypt-layer -1` to encrypt the last image like below?

```bash
skopeo copy --insecure-policy \
  --encryption-key provider:attestation-agent \
  --encrypt-layer -1 \
  docker://${Image} \
  docker://${EncImage}
```

Is there any suggestion?

comments.body:
Thanks for reaching out.

There is a fair bit of nuance to this. Generally, Skopeo tries _fairly hard_ to use data known to exist on the destination registry already. Exact duplicate layers within the same repository should always be reused; in other situations, whether a layer is “known to exist” is a bit hard to predict:
- It might depend on the registry implementation; some might find a match across the whole registry, not just the repository
- Skopeo / Podman might, over time, learn about differently-compressed duplicates (created e.g. using `podman pull && podman push` in some situations), and store that information in `…/blob-info-cache-v1.sqlite`. So, a first copy (of some kind) might behave differently from a subsequent one.
- Layer encryption does disable some of these features

Either way, whether a layer was copied or reused (“skipped”) should be clearly visible in the interactive progress bars.

---

I think the decision which layer to encrypt should be driven primarily by security objectives: in _most_ situations users wouldn’t want to give up on security objectives to just save a few seconds. But that’s up to the users to decide.

@mtrmac Thx for replying.

So whether a layer should be transfered depends on docker registry's response?

I'm using [distribution 2.8.3](https://github.com/distribution/distribution/tree/v2.8.3), I think it works very well with skopeo.

--------

In my thought if I encrypt all layers of the image, the `skopeo copy` will transfer all layer to the registry, none of the layer exist in the registry will be reused. Therefore, I use `--encrypt-layer` to encrypt layers of the image, hoping to reuse the existing data in the registry.

Right now, I use `skopeo inspect`&`yq` to get the layer information of the base image from registry, then calculate which layer should be encrypted, then use the param `--encrypt-layer x,y,z` to encrypt the image

It’s really best to test your specific workflow.

_When encrypting_, the upload will not reuse anything because the encrypted version (well, that particular encrypted version) does not exist yet, so there is nothing to check for reuse against. But once an image is encrypted, further copies should be reusing pre-existing data (with exactly the same encryption) on the destination — copying the same encrypted image twice to the same destination should, ideally, be only a metadata operation for the second copy.

@mtrmac 

I compared 2 images, one is the base, the other is build based on previous image and not encrypted yet.

Then I encrypted the 2nd one.

The script like follow

```bash
BaseImage="registry.svc/debian:bookswam-slim"
DestImage="registry.svc/helloworld:image"
DestEncImage="registry.svc/helloworld:image-encryped"

baseimage_layers=($(skopeo inspect docker://$BaseImage | yq '.Layers[]'))
destimage_layers=($(skopeo inspect docker://$DestImage | yq '.Layers[]'))

missing_layers=()
for i in "${!destimage_layers[@]}"; do
  layer="${destimage_layers[$i]}"
  if [[ ! " ${baseimage_layers[@]} " =~ " ${layer} " ]]; then
    missing_layers+=($i)
  fi
done
missing_layers_str=$(echo "${missing_layers[@]}" | tr ' ' ',')

skopeo copy --insecure-policy \
  --encryption-key provider:attestation-agent \
  --encrypt-layer ${missing_layers_str} \
  docker://${DestImage} \
  docker://${DestEncImage}
```

I did some tests, and it seems to be working fine.
